mnemonics.title: PROJECT SUCCESS Factors
mnemonics.content: S-U-C-C-E-S-S: Skilled Team, User Involvement, Clear Requirements, Communication, Executive Support, Sufficient Budget, Sound Planning

mnemonics.title: Failure Causes (The 6 Cs)
mnemonics.content: 6 Cs of Failure: Communication gaps, Cost issues, Complexity overload, Change without control, Capability gaps, Customer (stakeholder) misalignment

mnemonics.title: Iron Triangle Components
mnemonics.content: Iron Triangle = SCOPE + TIME + COST. If you change one, you must adjust the other two.

acronyms.usage: Key areas where results must be met for project success like clear requirements, executive support, user involvement
Hierarchical decomposition of project scope into manageable work packages
Traffic light system to indicate project health: Red=critical issues, Amber=concerns, Green=on track
Measurable values that demonstrate project effectiveness in achieving objectives
Standard guide for project management practices by PMI

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a triangle with three corners labeled Scope, Time, Cost. If you push one corner inward (reduce time), the other corners must adjust (reduce scope or increase cost). It's like a balloon - squeeze one side, another expands.
Draw a flowchart: Poor Planning → Unrealistic Schedule → Rushed Development → Poor Quality → Failed Project. Each box triggers the next problem.
Think of risk as rain clouds gathering (may or may not rain), while an issue is a puddle of water already formed (problem exists now).

analogies.analogy: Building a house: If you want a bigger house (scope) without more money, you must take longer time to build it. You can't change one without affecting others.
Ordering a pizza and asking for 'extra toppings' repeatedly until you have a completely different sandwich - the original scope gets lost.
Project success is like a car built in the factory on time. Product success is whether people actually buy and drive the car.
Risk management is like weather forecasting for a trip - you check for storms (risks), pack an umbrella (contingency), and have a backup plan (response).
